Review note (non-blocking):
- Consider `sudo rm -f` instead of `sudo rm` so a future chrony package that no longer ships `/etc/network/if-up.d/chrony` or `/etc/network/if-post-down.d/chrony` doesn't break the image build. The intent ("ensure these files are absent in the rootfs") is unchanged; `-f` just makes it declarative and matches the dominant idiom elsewhere in `sonic_debian_extension.j2`.

Thanks for the detailed review. Going through the questions:

**1. Build time impact** — Good question. We have instrumentation in place (per-`.deb` `lang-deps timing` log line in `sbom_fragment.py` and `scripts/build-timing-report.sh` for per-package wall-clock from log HEADER/FOOTER markers) but no published A/B benchmark yet. We'll run a back-to-back `ENABLE_SBOM=y` vs unset build on the same host and post the delta.

**2. Slave Dockerfile gating** — `cargo-auditable@0.6.4` and `rust-audit-info@0.5.4` are installed unconditionally, not gated by `ENABLE_SBOM`. The combined cost is small: ~24 MB against a 13.7 GB slave image (0.17%). We left it unconditional so a slave image rebuilt without `ENABLE_SBOM` doesn't silently break a subsequent `ENABLE_SBOM=y` build that reuses it.

**3. cargo-wrapper scope** — Only wraps `cargo build`. `files/build/cargo-wrapper` matches `build` explicitly in the `case` and falls through to `exec \"\$REAL\" \"\$@\"` for everything else (`test`, `clippy`, `fmt`, …). The wrapper docstring documents this.

**4. Scanner tool pinning** — Versions + SHA-256s are inlined in `scripts/install_sbom_tool.sh` today. Happy to lift them into a sidecar versions file for easier bumping if that's the preference.

**5. License map** — `README.sbom.md` has a \"Maintaining \`scripts/sbom_license_map.json\`\" section (lines 318-347) with the 5-step workflow for adding entries and the rationale for keeping it manually curated (false-positive risk on legally-meaningful license-string variants).

**6. syft OCI vs docker-archive** — SONiC's docker `.gz` files *are* OCI archives, not docker-archive. Spot-checked `target/docker-database.gz`: the inner tar has `blobs/sha256/...` at the top level (OCI layout), not `manifest.json` + `repositories` (docker-archive layout). The `oci-archive:` scheme is correct; `build_sbom.py:518-561` even handles the gzip wrap that syft can't read directly. That said, the last build did show ~6 `syft scan of oci-archive:... failed (rc=1)` warnings on a subset of containers — final cross-check still recovered 219,587 components so it wasn't catastrophic, but worth a separate look at which containers failed and why.

**7. VEX auto-extraction confidence** — Two-tier in `sbom_extract_vex_from_patches.py`: high-confidence CVE regex matches (explicit CVE-in-patch-header or Fixes: pattern) emit `status: not_affected` (suppresses in grype); loose mentions emit `status: under_investigation` which is a no-op for suppression — grype still surfaces the finding for human triage. So loose mentions cannot quietly hide a real vulnerability.

**Minor — \`dict[str, Any]\` PEP 585** — Not relevant in practice: only bookworm (3.11) and trixie (3.13) slaves are in active use; buster/bullseye Dockerfile edits were added for completeness but the SBOM emission paths don't run there.

Will follow up with the benchmark numbers.

Follow-up on the build-time question — we measured the SBOM-induced cost directly via temporary per-phase timing instrumentation added during development. That instrumentation was removed before final submission (only the optimizations themselves are shipping); the numbers below are from those measurement runs.

Per-phase attribution:

| Phase | Cost |
|---|---|
| Per-`.deb` lang-deps extraction (267 debs: `dpkg-deb -x` + `rust-audit-info` + `go version -m` + dist-info walk) | **~1m 45s** total |
| `collect_version_files` SBOM harvest (~70 invocations: 4 slaves × 2 phases + ~30 containers × 2 phases) | **~4m 04s** total |
| Aggregator × 3 ASIC variants | **~7m 30s** total |
| VEX patch-scan × 3 invocations | **~32s** total |
| **Sum of measured SBOM-induced cost** | **~14 min** |

Notes:
- Per-`.deb` extraction is cheap: 267 debs at avg 0.4s/deb. The kernel image alone is 49s (every kernel-module ELF gets walked); the rest are sub-second. Only 11 of 267 actually contained Rust/Go/Python attribution data — most extractions complete in well under 100ms.
- `collect_version_files` is the per-container/slave bulk `apt-cache show` + copyrights tar + lockfiles tar. Average 1.12s per invocation. Bulk `apt-cache show` dominates that step (avg 2.33s) since each container resolves ~1000 packages.
- Aggregator dominates the measurable SBOM cost. Per-variant breakdown for the primary build: `scanner_containers` 141s, `license_resolution` 75s, everything else <10s combined. Variants 2 and 3 short-circuit `scanner_containers` to ~15s via the per-archive SHA-256 cache, dropping their aggregator totals from ~230s to ~110s each.

- Consider a brief comment noting runtime installation to avoid future regressions.

@rameshraghupathy as discussed offline, this is a workaround rather than fixing the problem at source. The patch here specifically restarts interfaces-config.service but other oneshot services like config-setup.service are also vulnerable to this problem. The problem seems to be that with https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/25503, docker service is starting up early and failing due to some dependencies with ip6tables setup. All services that are dependent on docker will also fail (for eg: database, sonic-ztp, config-setup, interfaces-config). Some of them recover but all oneshot services will remain down.

> Nice fix for the first-boot race condition! A few suggestions:
> 
> **1. Hard failure on timeout is risky** If ip6tables isn't ready after 30s, `exit 1` permanently blocks Docker from starting. This is arguably worse than the original race (which could self-recover). Consider logging a warning and exiting 0 to let Docker attempt startup anyway, or add a systemd restart policy as fallback.
> 
> **2. No success logging** Adding a logger call on success would help debug boot timing issues later:
> 
> ```shell
> logger "docker-netfilter-ready: ip6tables ready after ${i}s"
> ```
> 
> **3. `-w 1` lock timeout may be too short** If another process holds the iptables lock during early boot, 1s wait could false-negative on each iteration. Consider `-w 5` to be more resilient to lock contention.
> 
> **4. Missing `set -e`** Not critical since the logic is simple, but good practice for shell scripts in systemd ExecStartPre paths.

@vvolam Thanks for the review. I agree with adding success/failure logging and increasing the per-command xtables wait from -w 1 to -w 5 to avoid false negatives due to early boot lock contention. For the timeout case, I prefer not to return success unconditionally because that would allow the original Docker failure cascade to happen again, but I can make the failure explicit in syslog so it is easier to diagnose. I will avoid set -e here because the script intentionally probes commands in an if condition and handles failures explicitly.

$(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v

> Can we try to mount the specific sysfs subdirectory, e.g.
> 
> $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /sys/class/mdio_bus/pci-mdio-2/mdio_access:/sys/class/mdio_bus/pci-mdio-2/mdio_access
> 
> and etc.

Pls check https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/issues/25142 
Removal of the flag has some side affects. If the sysfs path were to change due to some reason (say OIR of a module), one could possibly run into some problems (as noted in the issue). 
As of now, I dont think Cisco platforms have this issue for Gearbox devices but it could become an issue for future platforms if gearbox devices were on FRU-able modules.

> Can we try to mount the specific sysfs subdirectory, e.g.
> 
> $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /sys/class/mdio_bus/pci-mdio-2/mdio_access:/sys/class/mdio_bus/pci-mdio-2/mdio_access
> 
> and etc.

The mounts should be at the generic path like "/sys/class/mdio_bus" as different gearboxes has different subfolder to that they use for Nexthop device.
Also consider the fact that this implementation is vendor dependent, so it is not uniform across devices. What flags are we deciding to run the syncd on? The gbsyncd should get similar flags

> Can we try to mount the specific sysfs subdirectory, e.g.
> 
> $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /sys/class/mdio_bus/pci-mdio-2/mdio_access:/sys/class/mdio_bus/pci-mdio-2/mdio_access
> 
> and etc.

Can we add this? This will allow nexthop NH-5010 using agera2 gbsyncd to be able to do mdio read and writes.
```
  diff --git a/platform/components/docker-gbsyncd-agera2.mk b/platform/components/docker-gbsyncd-agera2.mk
  index 9dfa71c2d7..3f2944fe4c 100644
  --- a/platform/components/docker-gbsyncd-agera2.mk
  +++ b/platform/components/docker-gbsyncd-agera2.mk
  @@ -34,6 +34,8 @@ SONIC_INSTALL_DOCKER_DBG_IMAGES += $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2_DBG)

   $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_CONTAINER_NAME = gbsyncd
   $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += --cap-add=SYS_RAWIO --cap-add=SYS_ADMIN --cap-add=NET_ADMIN -t --security-opt apparmor=unconfined --security-opt="systempaths=unconfined"
  +$(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /sys/class/mdio_bus:/sys/class/mdio_bus
  +$(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /sys/devices:/sys/devices
   $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /host/machine.conf:/etc/machine.conf
   $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /etc/sonic:/etc/sonic:ro
   $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_AGERA2)_RUN_OPT += -v /host/warmboot:/var/warmboot

@zili11720 this should go to other SN5640 SKUs which are going to be merged soon. Please communicate and coordinate it with the owners. Same for SN6600

@zili11720 This PR introduces a regression that breaks port bring-up on SN5640 after reboot.
 
 The newly added comment line in `sai.profile`:

Disable tx squelch - keep transmit when the link is down

SAI_KEY_TX_SQUELCH_MODE=2

 
 causes `xcvrd` to crash repeatedly when parsing `/tmp/sai.profile`, because the parser does not skip comment / empty lines and tries `line.split('=')` directly:
 

ERR pmon#xcvrd: Failed to read from file /tmp/sai.profile - 
ValueError('not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 1)')

 
 As a result, `xcvrd` fails to read platform info, all transceivers are reported as **Not present**, and all ports stay operationally down after reboot:
 

$ show interfaces transceiver presence
Ethernet0    Not present
Ethernet1    Not present
...

 
 Verified workaround on the DUT (ports come up immediately):

sudo docker exec pmon sed -i '/^#/d' /tmp/sai.profile
sudo docker exec syncd sed -i '/^#/d' /tmp/sai.profile
sudo docker restart pmon

 
 Affected files:
 - `device/mellanox/x86_64-nvidia_sn5640-r0/Mellanox-SN5640-C512S2/sai.profile`
 - `device/mellanox/x86_64-nvidia_sn5640-r0/Mellanox-SN5640-C448O16/sai.profile`
 
 Could you submit a quick follow-up to either move the comment to end-of-line (e.g. `SAI_KEY_TX_SQUELCH_MODE=2  # Disable tx squelch ...`) or drop the comment? The proper fix should also be made in the `xcvrd` parser to skip `#` and blank lines

Hi @auspham — heads up on a couple of things while you rebase this on master (it currently shows `mergeable_state: dirty` because #27055 already removed gnoic).

**Test-side coordination**: dropping `/usr/local/bin/gnmic` from the ptf image will break `tests/gnmi/test_gnmic.py` on the next image roll. I have a sonic-mgmt PR adding a conditional_mark skip for that test that should land first: sonic-net/sonic-mgmt#24329 (tracking issue sonic-net/sonic-mgmt#24328).

**Orphan files**: the gnmic build block was the only consumer of these — they should also be removed in this PR, otherwise they linger as dead references:
- `dockers/docker-ptf/gocloud-patches/` (whole directory — only `0001-fix-aws-sdk-go-v2-pointer-api-changes.patch` was patched into the gocloud module by the gnmic build)
- `files/build/versions-public/default/versions-git`: the `https://github.com/openconfig/gnmic.git==883b7f9...` line
- `files/build/versions-public/dockers/docker-ptf/versions-deb-bookworm`: the `gnmic==0.43.0` line (it's a stale ci/build version-lock entry, not a real apt package)

Happy to send these as a follow-up PR or you can fold them in here, whichever you prefer.

> Thanks for the Trixie migration!
> 
> One question: the new `docker-gbsyncd-trixie.mk` adds a warmboot volume mount that isn't in the bookworm template:
> 
> ```
> $(DOCKER_GBSYNCD_BASE)_RUN_OPT += -v /host/warmboot:/var/warmboot
> ```
> 
> The bookworm equivalent (`docker-gbsyncd-bookworm.mk`) ends at the `/etc/sonic` mount, and the existing `docker-syncd-trixie.mk` template also doesn't include this warmboot mount.
> 
> Is this intentional for VPP warm restart support, or was it an accidental inclusion? If intentional, might be worth mentioning in the PR description.

@securely1g  Good catch - it was carried over from an older bookworm template and is unneeded. Removed.

@eddieruan-alibaba @dgsudharsan @vganesan-nokia for viz.

Validation of #26794 was done with a fresh full image build and DUT installation using the intended enablement path:

make ENABLE_FRR_TCMALLOC=y ...

Separately, the bookworm runtime package-name issue on `dockers/docker-fpm-frr/Dockerfile.j2` has also been noted in the line comment (`libgoogle-perftools4t64` vs `libgoogle-perftools4`).

However, the expected FRR tcmalloc runtime signals were absent in the running `bgp` container:

- `ldd /usr/lib/frr/zebra | grep -i tcmalloc` -> no output
- `ldd /usr/bin/vtysh | grep -i tcmalloc` -> no output
- `cat /proc/$(pidof zebra)/maps | grep tcmalloc` -> no output
- `vtysh -c "show tcmalloc stats"` -> `Unknown command`
- `dpkg -l | grep -i perftools` -> no output

So at least in this environment, building with `ENABLE_FRR_TCMALLOC=y` was not sufficient to produce a tcmalloc-enabled FRR/BGP runtime on the DUT.

> Validation of #26794 was done with a fresh full image build and DUT installation using the intended enablement path:
> 
> make ENABLE_FRR_TCMALLOC=y ...
> 
> Separately, the bookworm runtime package-name issue on `dockers/docker-fpm-frr/Dockerfile.j2` has also been noted in the line comment (`libgoogle-perftools4t64` vs `libgoogle-perftools4`).

Follow-up to the earlier validation note: with the two local validation changes below, the latest rebuild/retest is positive.

Local validation changes:
- `rules/config`: forced `ENABLE_FRR_TCMALLOC = y`
- `dockers/docker-fpm-frr/Dockerfile.j2`: `libgoogle-perftools4t64` -> `libgoogle-perftools4`

With that setup, the rebuilt image shows working FRR tcmalloc support:

- `ldd /usr/lib/frr/zebra | grep -i tcmalloc` -> `libtcmalloc.so.4`
- `ldd /usr/bin/vtysh | grep -i tcmalloc` -> `libtcmalloc.so.4`
- `cat /proc/$(pidof zebra)/maps | grep tcmalloc` -> shows `libtcmalloc.so.4.5.10`
- `vtysh -c "show tcmalloc stats"` -> prints tcmalloc stats for daemons including `zebra`
- `dpkg -l | grep -i perftools` -> `libgoogle-perftools4` installed

So the functionality does work with those local changes applied.

The separate bookworm runtime package-name issue noted in the line comment still stands.

The remaining open question is whether the PR’s intended enablement path (`make ENABLE_FRR_TCMALLOC=y ...`) works reliably without forcing `ENABLE_FRR_TCMALLOC = y` in `rules/config`.

> @rameshraghupathy Is this a regression or behavior change?

@vvolam This is a regression fix, not an intended behavior change. On SmartSwitch lit-mode, databasedpuX instances are expected to be started before PMON because PMON depends on DPU DB availability when DPUs are present. Previously they were effectively coupled with the main database startup path. After the recent decoupling work, host services/containers no longer bounce when databasedpuX bounces, but that also means we need an explicit boot-time mechanism to start/order database@dpuX. This PR adds that missing startup ordering by making PMON Wants= and After= all database@dpuX.service instances. So the intent is to restore the expected SmartSwitch lit-mode boot behavior without reintroducing runtime coupling.

Should this sharpd be enabled for testing purpose only, instead of default in SONiC? 

In deployment, an extra unused and unmonitored process could be a headache. 

If we want to enable sharpd in deployment, we need to add monitoring to this process to avoid this guy behave bad, for example leaking memory.

> I am not a big fan to enable this process in production.
> 
> Can we enable it for testing only.

Discussed in WG meeting, since the following two flags are off, sharpd would not be launched in production.

autostart=false
autorestart=false

So it is ok.

Note: only the crash signal overrides (SIGSEGV, SIGILL, SIGBUS, SIGXFSZ, SIGABRT) are removed. The remaining signals are retained because they have useful application-level handlers:

- **SIGTERM** → graceful shutdown (EVStop)
- **SIGINT** → abrupt exit with cleanup
- **SIGUSR1** → decrease debug log level
- **SIGUSR2** → increase debug log level
- **SIGHUP** → reopen log files (for logrotate)

The crash signal handler was catching fatal signals, logging a text backtrace, then calling `exit(sig)` — which suppressed core dump generation. With the default kernel handlers restored, crashes will now produce proper core dumps for post-mortem debugging with GDB.
This PR includes the 0006 defer-redis patch from #26699 and should be merged after #26699.

Since https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-swss/pull/4451 got closed, pasting the design proposal here for later reference..

>>>>>
Fundamentally, I consider zmq as Infra/PI piece, and coupling this with platforms/HWSKUs creates duplication/complex code.

I was going through of this PR set and the ASIC_DB persistence PRs (https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/pull/1801, https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/pull/1819). I think there's an opportunity to significantly simplify the design. Wanted to share my thoughts.. up for discussion.

Core observation: The current design couples three independent decisions into one trigger:

ZMQ transport ON/OFF
ASIC_DB persistence ON/OFF
context_config.json required
On DPU, coupling these made sense — DPUs don't need ASIC_DB, and they already ship context_config.json. But for non-DPU switches, this coupling creates the problems we're solving with -g, ASIC_DB restore PRs, and per-hwsku JSON files.. we have bunch of platforms which dont ship with context_config.json.. Also this PR set had a need to add context_config.json for vs..(JFYI, in the past, we had built some similar platform dependency in Chassis for Midplane-IPs and later had to move it out of platform specific code)..

Proposed simplification (4 changes):

ContextConfig.cpp — Change defaults from ipc:///tmp/zmq_ep to tcp://127.0.0.1:5555. Every platform
that ships context_config.json already uses TCP localhost. The IPC default looks like dead code (please correct me if I am wrong). This eliminates the need for context_config.json just to get ZMQ transport working.

Syncd.cpp — Tighten the DisabledRedisClient guard from if (zmq_enable && !VS) to if
(zmq_enable && !VS && isDpu). Non-DPU switches get ZMQ transport with ASIC_DB preserved — tooling
(routeCheck, SNMP, show commands) works immediately without any ASIC_DB restore mechanism. To also
remove syncd's Redis write from the hot path, above PRs for async persistence is still valuable but
becomes a pure performance optimization, not a correctness requirement. Much cleaner scope for those PRs.

orchagent.sh / syncd_init_common.sh — Read a CONFIG_DB knob (zmq_southbound or similar). If enabled, pass -z zmq_sync. Keep the existing elif switch_type == dpu as fallback for backward compatibility — DPU vendors can migrate to the CONFIG_DB knob at their own pace.

No -g flag needed — I assume this is to keep gearbox communication channel via redis? if so, Gearbox is naturally handled by process separation (syncd gets -z, gbsyncd doesn't — they're separate processes with independent command lines). Orchagent still reads context_config.json for gearbox topology (guids), and selectively enables zmq for the required context.

Result: Any switch — can enable ZMQ with one CONFIG_DB key. No context_config.json needed. No ASIC_DB breakage. DPUs continue working unchanged. And https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/pull/1801, https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/pull/1819 can focus purely on async perf optimization rather than correctness restoration.

I believe this is caused by [sonic-swss PR #4394](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-swss/pull/4394) which added -lnexthopgroup to fpmsyncd's link dependencies and updated swss's own CI pipeline to download the libnexthopgroup deb, but did not update the sairedis CI pipeline accordingly. So when sairedis CI compiles swss from master, it cannot find the library.

I've opened a PR https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/pull/1908 to fix this. It adds the libnexthopgroup deb download and installation to sairedis CI pipeline files.

The CI failure is real. Investigating if it is related to trixie docker upgrade.

## CI Failure Root Cause Analysis

The kvmtest-t0 failure (`sflow/test_sflow.py::TestSflowCollector::test_sflow_config`) is **not caused by this PR's changes**.

### Root Cause

`system-health.service` crashes on trixie VS images due to a missing file:
```
FileNotFoundError: Metadata file /etc/sonic/vs_chassis_metadata.json not found
```

This prevents `SYSTEM_READY|SYSTEM_STATE` from being set in STATE_DB. hsflowd waits for system-ready before reading CONFIG_DB, so it never initializes — causing the 240s timeout in `wait_until_hsflowd_ready()`.

### Evidence

- The sflow container itself works correctly — manually setting `SYSTEM_READY` in STATE_DB immediately unblocks hsflowd
- This is a pre-existing trixie host-level bug, not related to the sflow container upgrade
- sflow tests are blanket-skipped on other PRs via `tests_mark_conditions.yaml` (sonic-mgmt#21701); this PR's impacted-area analysis forced them to run

### Tracking

Filed #26429 to track the system-health fix. Working on a PR to address it.
PR checker failure is legit, will be fixed by https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/26446

## Root Cause Analysis: sflow test failures on trixie

### Problem
All sflow tests fail on the trixie sflow container because `hsflowd` cannot load its SONiC integration module (`mod_sonic.so`).

### Root Cause: libhiredis SONAME mismatch

`hsflowd` is built as `SONIC_MAKE_DEBS` in `rules/sflow.mk`, meaning it is **only compiled once in the bookworm build slave**. Its `mod_sonic.so` shared library module links dynamically against `libhiredis.so.0.14` (the bookworm SONAME).

**Trixie does not ship `libhiredis0.14`** — it only has `libhiredis1.1.0` (SONAME `libhiredis.so.1.1.0`). When hsflowd tries to `dlopen("mod_sonic.so")` at runtime inside the trixie container, the dynamic linker cannot resolve `libhiredis.so.0.14`, and the module **fails to load silently**.

Without `mod_sonic.so`, hsflowd runs as a vanilla sFlow daemon with zero SONiC integration — no Redis connection, no CONFIG_DB reads, no `hsflowd.auto` generation. The test `wait_until_hsflowd_ready()` waits 240s for collectors in `/etc/hsflowd.auto` that will never appear.

### Why bookworm passes 100%
The bookworm sflow container has `libhiredis0.14` installed. `mod_sonic.so` loads successfully, connects to Redis, reads sflow config, and generates `hsflowd.auto` promptly.

### Why other trixie containers work
Other native-code packages (e.g., `libswsscommon`, `swss`) are `SONIC_DPKG_DEBS`, which the build system compiles in **both** bookworm and trixie slaves. Their trixie variants (in `target/debs/trixie/`) link against `libhiredis.so.1.1.0`. There is no `target/debs/trixie/hsflowd*.deb`.

### Verification
```
$ ldd mod_sonic.so | grep hiredis
libhiredis.so.0.14 => not found

$ docker run --rm debian:trixie apt-get install -y libhiredis0.14
E: Unable to locate package libhiredis0.14
```

### Fix
`hsflowd` (and potentially `sflowtool`, `psample`) need to be rebuilt in the trixie build slave so `mod_sonic.so` links against trixie's `libhiredis.so.1.1.0`. This likely requires converting from `SONIC_MAKE_DEBS` to `SONIC_DPKG_DEBS` in `rules/sflow.mk`, or adding an equivalent trixie slave build mechanism.

### Note on system-health (#26446)
The system-health crash (chassis.py `FileNotFoundError`) is a **separate, real bug** affecting all non-chassis VS images since PR #18512 (April 2024). It prevents `SYSTEM_READY` from being set in STATE_DB. However, it is **not the cause of sflow test failures** — `mod_sonic.so` never loads, so hsflowd never reaches the `SYSTEM_READY` check.

## Correction to RCA

My previous analysis about `libhiredis` SONAME mismatch was **wrong for CI builds**.

The CI build has two passes (`BLDENV=bookworm` then `BLDENV=trixie`). In the trixie pass, hsflowd is built from scratch in the trixie slave container, linking `mod_sonic.so` against trixie's `libhiredis.so.1.1.0`. Build log confirms:
```
[ building ] [ target/debs/trixie/hsflowd_2.0.51-26_amd64.deb ]
[ finished ] [ target/debs/trixie/hsflowd_2.0.51-26_amd64.deb ]
```

### Revised RCA: SYSTEM_READY timeout + hsflowd restart

The actual failure chain:

1. **system-health crashes** on VS due to `chassis.py` bug (#18512) — `FileNotFoundError` for `/etc/sonic/vs_chassis_metadata.json`
2. `SYSTEM_READY|SYSTEM_STATE` is **never set to UP** in STATE_DB
3. When the test applies sflow config, `sflowmgrd` calls `service hsflowd restart` (see `sonic-swss/cfgmgr/sflowmgr.cpp:60`)
4. The freshly restarted hsflowd connects to DBs and enters `HSP_SONIC_STATE_WAIT_READY`, waiting for `SYSTEM_READY|SYSTEM_STATE = UP`
5. Since SYSTEM_READY is never set, hsflowd waits the full **180 seconds** (`HSP_SONIC_DEFAULT_WAITREADY_SECS`)
6. After 180s timeout, hsflowd proceeds to read config and generate `hsflowd.auto`
7. The test's `wait_until_hsflowd_ready()` has a **240-second timeout** — leaving only ~60s margin after the 180s wait
8. On trixie, the additional overhead (service restart time, DB connection setup, config processing) pushes the total past 240s

### Why bookworm passes
Bookworm has the same system-health crash (chassis.py bug exists since April 2024). The 180s wait should occur on both. However, bookworm's container startup and service restart may be slightly faster, keeping the total under 240s. Alternatively, bookworm's sflowmgrd may behave slightly differently in timing.

### Fix
PR #26446 (system-health fix) is the correct fix — it prevents the `FileNotFoundError` crash, allowing system-health to set `SYSTEM_READY=UP`, which lets hsflowd skip the 180s wait entirely.

### Note on libhiredis
The libhiredis SONAME issue (`libhiredis.so.0.14` vs `libhiredis.so.1.1.0`) is still a **real concern for single-pass builds** (e.g., local builds with only `BLDENV=bookworm`). If someone builds a bookworm-only image that includes docker-sflow-trixie, the bookworm-compiled hsflowd would fail to load `mod_sonic.so` in the trixie container. The dual-pass CI build avoids this, but the risk exists for non-CI build workflows.

**Overall: ✅ Approve with minor suggestions** — Clean, well-tested infrastructure refactoring.

### Strengths
- **Follows the existing DNX submodule pattern exactly** — The `.dep` file changes mirror the `saibcm-modules-dnx` submodule pattern (`_SMDEP_FILES`, `_SMDEP_PATHS`). This proves the caching mechanism already works.
- **Massive repo hygiene win** — Removing 633 inline files / 388K lines from sonic-buildimage significantly reduces clone/fetch size.
- **Excellent HW verification** — Tested on real hardware (Arista 7050CX3-32S / XGS chip), all 15 SONiC containers healthy, knet modules loaded correctly. 
- **Submodule target validated** — Branch `sdk-6.5.34-xgs` exists on `sonic-net/saibcm-modules` and the pinned commit `ead12b48` is valid.

### Issues Found

**⚠️ CRLF Line Endings in `sai-modules.dep`**
The diff shows `\r\n` (Windows CRLF) line endings in the new `sai-modules.dep` content, while the original used `\n` (Unix LF). Makefiles can be sensitive to line endings — while GNU Make on Linux generally handles CRLF, this is inconsistent with the rest of the repo. Please convert to LF:
```bash
sed -i 's/\r$//' platform/broadcom/sai-modules.dep
```

@bhouse-nexthop If I'm reading [this](https://trustedcomputinggroup.org/wp-content/uploads/Registry-of-Reserved-TPM-2.0-Handles-and-Localities-Version-1.2-Revision-1.00_pub.pdf) right, 0x81010000-0x810100ff can be used to store the endorsement primary key (section 2.3.1), so maybe the use of 0x81010001 is fine here?

> @bhouse-nexthop If I'm reading [this](https://trustedcomputinggroup.org/wp-content/uploads/Registry-of-Reserved-TPM-2.0-Handles-and-Localities-Version-1.2-Revision-1.00_pub.pdf) right, 0x81010000-0x810100ff can be used to store the endorsement primary key (section 2.3.1), so maybe the use of 0x81010001 is fine here? I mean it looks pretty clear to me: Its reserved for endorsement primary keys only. The reason there are 256 slots are for different key types (RSA vs ECC, etc). I'm honestly surprised the TPM would even allow you to use it in that manner since this is an auto-generated key handle when you call `tpm2_createek`. Let me find the reference to the actual slot, I think they started using it in later versions.
Not finding anything superseding this: https://trustedcomputinggroup.org/wp-content/uploads/TCG-TPM-v2.0-Provisioning-Guidance-Published-v1r1.pdf
